one_line_takeaway: Representing PE files as API/string-annotated function call graphs and constraining the GCN+classifier to non-negative weights makes the detector provably monotonic against benign-code-injection evasion, reaching 100% robust accuracy on the authors' crypter-based attack where non-negative MalConv scores 0.08%.
key_finding: On the clean test set Mal2GCN beats MalConv in every setting (97.44% vs 94.98% accuracy). Under the source-code injection attack the gap is dramatic: Mal2GCN+ (non-negative) achieves 100% robust accuracy vs MalConv+'s 0.08%, and adversarial training alone is insufficient (Mal2GCN-AT only 75.20%). A counterintuitive ablation: non-negativity must sit on the GCN layers, not the classifier — Mal2GCN-GCN+ reaches 98.85% robust accuracy while Mal2GCN-GClf+ collapses to 8.55%, BELOW the 40.05% unconstrained baseline. Even unconstrained Mal2GCN needs >500% source overhead to fully bypass vs <50% for MalConv, evidence that FCG representation is intrinsically more robust than raw-byte representation. Caveat on the headline 100%: it is measured against the paper's own 2000 samples from a single crypter source, and the theoretical monotonicity guarantee is conditional (holds only for benign-additive attacks AND only if the clean malware was already classified correctly).
core_contribution: Mal2GCN, a Windows malware detector that represents each executable as a Function Call Graph (FCG) whose nodes (functions) carry a 1000-dim Bag-of-Words feature vector over the top-500 APIs and top-500 referenced strings, fed to a two-layer Graph Convolutional Network plus a Graph Classifier (GClf). The paper's distinctive, most defensible novelty is the observation that applying non-negative weight training to Mal2GCN makes the WHOLE model a monotonically non-decreasing function, whereas the prior Non-Negative MalConv of Fleshman et al. [15] cannot be made fully monotonic because its byte-embedding layer breaks monotonicity even with non-negative weights. Because Mal2GCN contains only fully-connected and pooling layers over non-negative BoW inputs and ReLU/sigmoid activations, restricting all GCN and GClf weights to be non-negative yields the guarantee that adding benign APIs/strings can only INCREASE the malware probability. Two further contributions: (2) a black-box source-code-level adversarial malware generator that injects benign-looking code segments (dead-code opaque predicates + benign block content) into malware source; and (3) a new 58,157-PE dataset. NOTE: the non-negative training METHOD and MalConv are prior work (Fleshman [15] and Raff [20] respectively), not this paper's inventions; the paper adapts them.
threat_model: Black-box, real-world adversary who possesses their OWN malware source code (not just the compiled binary) and no access to the detector's parameters, only its label output. The adversary's goal is evasion: make a functioning malware sample be classified benign by a static DL-based detector. The allowed perturbation is ADDITIVE and functionality-preserving: inject benign-looking code (benign API calls, benign strings, junk/dead computations guarded by always-false opaque predicates) into arbitrary source-code locations, then recompile with optimization disabled so injected code survives. Unlike Lp-bounded image attacks, there is no bound on size, content, or location of the perturbation. Feature removal is disallowed (would break functionality). The monotonicity defense targets exactly this benign-additive class; the paper explicitly does NOT defend against attacks that alter malicious behavior itself, packing, dynamic library loading, TEB parsing, or runtime string decryption (listed as limitations).
limitations: As a static detector Mal2GCN inherits static-analysis weaknesses the authors acknowledge: custom packers/UPX (needs pre-unpacking), dynamic library loading and runtime API resolution, function-address discovery via Thread Environment Block parsing, and encrypted-strings-decrypted-at-runtime — all of which change the effective FCG at runtime and are outside the defended threat class. The monotonicity guarantee only covers benign-additive perturbations; an attacker who alters the malware's malicious pattern is not blocked. The 100% robust accuracy is against a single-crypter (Lime Crypter, C#/.NET) attack family with compiler optimization deliberately disabled, so generalization to other malware sources/languages and to optimized builds is untested. Non-negativity costs ~1% clean accuracy (97.44 to 96.41). Reliance on IDA Pro for FCG/feature extraction (0.28s/app) is heavy, though the authors note graphs could be produced by faster tooling. Future work (Emu2GCN) proposes emulation to address static limitations.

datasets_used: Self-collected dataset of 58,157 PE files (30,975 malware / 27,182 benign); split train 38,977 / validation 9,590 / test 9,590

Malware sourced from VirusShare, filtered to >=20 VirusTotal detections, labeled by AVClass into 300+ families (top families incl. Installerex, Domaiq, Firseria, Loadmoney, Bettersurf, Bladabindi, Emotet)

Benign from fresh Windows XP-to-10 system executables (exe/dll/sys, 32- and 64-bit) plus crawled freeware (e.g. freewarefiles), installers unpacked via UniExtractor, filtered to <=2 VirusTotal detections
2,000 adversarial malware generated by injecting adversarial code into the Lime Crypter (C#) source, benign APIs/strings mined from Windows system .NET executables
quantitative_results: Clean test accuracy (Table 2): MalConv 94.98%, MalConv-AT 95.72%, MalConv+ 90.50%, Mal2GCN 97.44%, Mal2GCN-AT 98.15%, Mal2GCN+ 96.41%
Clean test F1 (Table 2): Mal2GCN 97.80%, Mal2GCN-AT 98.41%, Mal2GCN+ 96.87%, MalConv 95.59%
AUC: Mal2GCN 0.993, Mal2GCN-AT 0.993, Mal2GCN+ 0.986, MalConv 0.988, MalConv-AT 0.989, MalConv+ 0.978
Robust accuracy on 2000 adversarial samples (Table 4): MalConv 7.15% / AT 35.65% / + 0.08%; Mal2GCN 40.05% / AT 75.20% / + 100%
Non-negativity placement ablation (Table 5): Mal2GCN 40.05%, Mal2GCN-GClf+ 8.55%, Mal2GCN-GCN+ 98.85%, Mal2GCN+ 100% robust accuracy
Source-code overhead to fully bypass: MalConv <50%, Mal2GCN >500% (Figure 12)
Feature/vocabulary size: 1000 per node (top-500 APIs + top-500 strings via random forest)
Best hyperparameters (Table 1): 2 GCN layers (sizes 500, 250), 1 GClf hidden layer size 64, avg readout, lr 0.008
Per-app runtime (Table 3): IDA Pro call-graph+feature extraction 0.28s, DGL graph generation 0.025s, Mal2GCN classification 0.014s
baselines_compared: MalConv (Raff et al. [20]) — raw-byte CNN detector
Non-Negative MalConv (Fleshman et al. [15]) — MalConv+ with non-negative weight restriction
Adversarially-trained variants (MalConv-AT, Mal2GCN-AT) trained on 2000 injected adversarial samples
Internal Mal2GCN ablations: Mal2GCN-GCN+ and Mal2GCN-GClf+ (non-negativity on only one sub-network)
